era_context: A pivotal year marking the death of Darius I and the succession of Xerxes I to the Persian throne. In Greece, the city-states are increasingly aware of Persian military power and are beginning to prepare for a potential invasion. In Egypt, a rebellion against Persian rule is occurring. In China, the Spring and Autumn Period continues with interstate conflicts. This year represents a major transition in the ancient world.
geographic_coverage_gaps: China (limited specific records for this year)

events.title: Death of Darius I and Succession of Xerxes I
events.description: Darius I, the Great King of Persia who had ruled for 36 years (522–486 BCE), died and was succeeded by his son Xerxes I. Darius had expanded the Persian Empire to its greatest territorial extent and had attempted to conquer Greece, resulting in the failed Marathon campaign of 490 BCE. Xerxes inherited a vast empire but also faced immediate challenges, including rebellions in Egypt and Babylon. Xerxes would later launch the second, much larger invasion of Greece in 480 BCE. The succession was smooth, as Xerxes had been designated as heir, but the new reign would be marked by military campaigns and internal consolidation.
key_figures: Atossa (mother of Xerxes, widow of Darius)
events.certainty_note: The death of Darius I and succession of Xerxes I in 486 BCE is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. The date is secure from Herodotus and other Greek historians, and is consistent with Persian chronology.

events.title: Egyptian Rebellion Against Persian Rule
events.description: Following the death of Darius I, Egypt rebelled against Persian rule. The rebellion was led by Egyptian nationalists who sought to restore Egyptian independence after decades of Persian domination. Xerxes I responded by sending military forces to suppress the rebellion. The Egyptian revolt was eventually crushed, and Egypt remained under Persian control, though the rebellion demonstrated the fragility of Persian rule in conquered territories and the persistence of local resistance to foreign domination.
key_figures: Egyptian rebels (names not recorded in surviving sources)
events.certainty_note: The Egyptian rebellion is attested in Herodotus and later sources, but the precise date is uncertain. Most scholars place it in 486 or 485 BCE, following Darius I's death. The details of the rebellion are sparse in surviving sources.
cross_references: 486-001 (Death of Darius I and succession of Xerxes)

events.title: Babylonian Rebellion Against Persian Rule
events.description: Following the death of Darius I, Babylon also rebelled against Persian rule. The rebellion was led by Babylonian nationalists who sought to restore Babylonian independence. Xerxes I responded by sending military forces to suppress the rebellion. The Babylonian revolt was crushed, and Babylon remained under Persian control. Xerxes' harsh response to the Babylonian rebellion (including the destruction of the temple of Marduk, according to some sources) demonstrated the new Great King's determination to maintain control over the empire's core territories.
key_figures: Babylonian rebels (names not recorded in surviving sources)
events.certainty_note: The Babylonian rebellion is attested in ancient sources, but the precise date is uncertain. Most scholars place it in 486 or 485 BCE, following Darius I's death. The details of the rebellion and Xerxes' response are debated among scholars.
cross_references: 486-001 (Death of Darius I and succession of Xerxes)

events.title: Themistocles' Rise to Prominence in Athens
events.description: Themistocles, an Athenian statesman and military strategist, rose to prominence in Athens during the mid-480s BCE. He advocated for the development of Athenian naval power and the construction of a strong fleet, recognizing the Persian threat. Themistocles' influence grew as the Athenian Assembly became increasingly concerned about Persian intentions. His policies would shape Athenian strategy in the coming years and would prove crucial in the Greek response to the second Persian invasion in 480 BCE.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War

events.certainty_note: Themistocles' rise to prominence is attested in ancient sources, but the precise chronology is uncertain. Most scholars place his emergence as a major political figure in the mid-480s BCE, though some sources suggest slightly different dates.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating and details of the Egyptian and Babylonian rebellions are disputed. Some sources place these rebellions in 485 or 484 BCE rather than 486 BCE. The extent of Xerxes' involvement in suppressing these rebellions is debated; some scholars argue that the rebellions may have been suppressed by subordinate commanders rather than by Xerxes himself. Additionally, the claim that Xerxes destroyed the temple of Marduk in Babylon is disputed; some sources suggest this occurred later or not at all. The narrative of Themistocles' rise to prominence may be shaped by later Athenian historiography that emphasizes his role in the eventual Greek victory over Persia.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 486 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Herodotus, who provides information about the succession of Xerxes and the rebellions in Egypt and Babylon. However, Herodotus was writing decades after these events and relied on Greek informants, which may have introduced bias. No contemporary Persian sources survive for this year. The Egyptian and Babylonian rebellions are attested in Greek sources but are not well-documented in surviving Egyptian or Babylonian sources. The focus on Themistocles' rise reflects the Athenian bias in surviving sources; events in other Greek cities or in the Persian Empire are less well-documented. The interpretation of these events is shaped by later historiographic frameworks that emphasize the conflict between Greece and Persia.

graph_edges.note: Xerxes I's succession led to his decision to launch the second invasion of Greece
The Egyptian rebellion was a response to the transition in Persian leadership
The Babylonian rebellion was a response to the transition in Persian leadership
Themistocles' advocacy for naval power influenced Athenian strategy in the second Persian invasion
